Glossary:
# Glossary

Quick definitions for every acronym and term used across the Computer Systems notes.

## A–C

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **ALU** | Arithmetic Logic Unit — the circuit inside the CPU that performs addition, subtraction, bitwise operations, and comparisons |
| **ASLR** | Address Space Layout Randomization — randomizes where code, stack, and heap are placed in memory to make buffer overflow attacks harder |
| **ASM / Assembly** | Human-readable representation of machine code; one assembly instruction = one CPU instruction |
| **Branch Predictor** | A circuit that guesses whether a conditional jump will be taken, keeping the pipeline full |

## D–F

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **DRAM** | Dynamic Random Access Memory — the technology behind RAM sticks; stores each bit as charge in a capacitor that must be refreshed ~64 ms |
| **ELF** | Executable and Linkable Format — the file format for executables on Linux |
| **External Fragmentation** | Free memory exists in aggregate, but is split into pieces too small to satisfy a request; the reason segmentation lost to paging |
| **Futex** | Fast Userspace muTEX — a Linux kernel primitive for efficient locking; fast path stays in userspace with atomics, slow path calls the kernel to sleep/wake threads |
| **FTL** | Flash Translation Layer — the SSD controller firmware that maps logical block addresses to physical NAND pages, handles wear leveling and garbage collection |

## G–J

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **GOT** | Global Offset Table — a table in dynamically-linked executables that holds the actual addresses of external functions; populated by the dynamic linker at load time |
| **IPC** | Instructions Per Clock — how many instructions the CPU completes per clock cycle; modern CPUs achieve 2-4 IPC through superscalar execution |
| **IR** | Intermediate Representation — a compiler-internal format between source code and machine code; LLVM IR and GCC GIMPLE are examples |

## K–M

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **KPTI** | Kernel Page Table Isolation — a mitigation for the Meltdown vulnerability; the kernel's memory is unmapped from userspace page tables, so every syscall must switch page tables (adding 100-500+ cycles) |
| **LAPIC** | Local Advanced Programmable Interrupt Controller — the per-core interrupt controller that handles timer interrupts for the scheduler |
| **LRU** | Least Recently Used — a page replacement policy that evicts the page accessed furthest in the past; approximated by the Clock algorithm in real OSes |
| **LSB / MSB** | Least/Most Significant Byte — the byte at the lowest/highest positional weight in a multi-byte number |
| **MESI** | Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid — the four states of the cache coherence protocol that keeps all CPU cores' caches consistent |
| **MLFQ** | Multi-Level Feedback Queue — a scheduling algorithm with multiple priority queues; threads start at high priority, are demoted if they use full quanta (CPU-bound), stay high if they block on I/O (interactive) |
| **MMU** | Memory Management Unit — the hardware that translates virtual addresses to physical addresses using the page table and TLB |
| **μop** | Micro-operation — the CPU decodes complex x86 instructions into simpler internal μops that the execution units actually process |

## N–P

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **NAND Flash** | The storage technology behind SSDs; stores bits by trapping charge in floating-gate transistors; organized as pages (write unit) within blocks (erase unit) |
| **NUMA** | Non-Uniform Memory Access — multi-socket systems where each CPU has faster access to its own local RAM than to another socket's RAM |
| **NX / DEP** | No-eXecute / Data Execution Prevention — marks memory pages as non-executable so attackers can't run code injected into data buffers |
| **PE** | Portable Executable — the file format for executables on Windows (EXE and DLL) |
| **PLT** | Procedure Linkage Table — a table of stubs for dynamically-linked functions; on first call, the stub invokes the dynamic linker to resolve the address |
| **PTE** | Page Table Entry — one entry in the page table; stores the physical frame number, permissions (read/write/execute), and flags (present, dirty, accessed) |

## Q–S

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **RAID** | Redundant Array of Inexpensive Disks — combining multiple disks for speed (striping), reliability (mirroring), or both (parity) |
| **ROB** | Reorder Buffer — a CPU structure that tracks in-flight instructions and retires them in program order; Zen 4: 320 entries, Raptor Lake: 512 entries |
| **SMT** | Simultaneous Multi-Threading — Intel's marketing term is Hyper-Threading; a single physical core exposes two logical cores by duplicating architectural state (registers) while sharing execution units |
| **SSE / AVX** | Streaming SIMD Extensions / Advanced Vector Extensions — x86 instruction set extensions for vector operations (SIMD = Single Instruction, Multiple Data) |
| **SSO** | Small String Optimization — `std::string` stores short strings inside the object itself (no heap allocation); typically 15-23 characters depending on standard library |

## T–Z

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **TLB** | Translation Lookaside Buffer — a small, fast cache inside the MMU that stores recent virtual→physical address translations; a TLB miss requires walking the page table (4 memory accesses for 4-level paging) |
| **TCO** | Tail Call Optimization — when a function's last action is calling another function, the compiler reuses the current stack frame instead of creating a new one; prevents stack overflow in recursive functions |
| **UHT** | Unreal Header Tool — UE's custom pre-compilation tool that parses UCLASS/UPROPERTY/UFUNCTION macros and generates reflection code |
| **VMA** | Virtual Memory Area — the kernel's internal data structure (`vm_area_struct` on Linux) that describes one contiguous region of a process's virtual address space |
| **vDSO** | Virtual Dynamic Shared Object — a small shared library the kernel maps into every process; contains frequently-called syscall implementations that run entirely in userspace (e.g., `gettimeofday`) |

## See Also

- [[From Transistor to Running Program]] — The big-picture overview
- [[Computer Systems/Learning Path]] — Recommended reading order
- [[DeepVault Guide]] — Full vault navigation
DeepVault Guide:
# DeepVault Guide

Welcome to DeepVault — an interactive learning fortress for mastering C++, Unreal Engine, and the computer systems beneath them.

## How This Works

Every folder on the dashboard contains **Learn** notes and **Quiz** exercises:

| Mode | What It Does |
|---|---|
| **Learn** | The full vault note, rendered exactly as it appears in Obsidian. Read, scroll the right-side table of contents, click wiki links to jump between notes. |
| **Quiz** | 5-10 randomized questions per note — multiple choice and text input. Instant feedback. Scores saved locally on your device. |

### Tracking Your Progress

Progress is based on **quiz scores only** — reading a note does not count. Here's how it works:

| Status | What It Means |
|---|---|
| **NOT STARTED** | You haven't taken the quiz yet |
| **IN PROGRESS** | At least 1 attempt, but best score is below 80% |
| **COMPLETED** | You scored 80% or higher on at least one attempt |

The folder header shows your overall completion percentage. Quiz scores and attempt history are saved locally via localStorage — they never leave your device.

**Wiki links** like [[04 — CPU Architecture]] connect notes to each other. Click any link to jump to that note's Learn page.

**Recent notes** appear on the dashboard below your folders — quick jump back to what you were studying.

---

## Master Study Order

This vault is designed to be read in order. Each note builds on the ones before it. See the [[Learning Path]] for the full reading order — this is just the big-picture map.

### Layer 1 — Computer Systems (start here)

Without understanding the hardware, C++ is just magic words. Start with the physical reality.

Covering: bits and bytes → assembly → CPU → memory hierarchy → virtual memory → storage → DMA → networking → boot process → GPU → interrupts → system software → concurrency. Over 50 notes from transistor to thread pool.

Start with [[01 — Bits, Bytes & Number Systems]] and follow the [[Learning Path — Computer Systems|Computer Systems Learning Path]].

### Layer 2 — C++ Fundamentals

Once you understand the hardware, learn the language that runs on it.

45 notes across 6 phases: Primer (absolute beginner) → Syntax & Types → Memory → OOP & Generics → Modern C++ → Engine Bridge. Every note has exercises.

Start with [[01 — Primer/00 — Primer Overview|C++ Primer]] and follow the [[Learning Path — C++ Fundamentals|C++ Learning Path]].

### Layer 3 — Game Math

The mathematics that make 3D worlds possible.

41 notes across 10 subfolders: Trigonometry → Matrices → Quaternions → Collision Detection → Physics Simulation → Animation → Navigation → Rendering → Procedural Generation → Audio & Networking → Geometric Algebra → UE Bridge. Framework-agnostic (pure C++ structs, not UE-specific).

Start with [[From Trigonometry to 3D Transformations]] and follow the [[Learning Path — Game Math|Game Math Learning Path]].

### Layer 4 — Unreal Engine Core

How UE turns C++ into a game engine.

14 notes: UHT & Macros → Pointer Ecosystem → String Types → Casting → Containers → Templates → Move Semantics.

Start with [[UHT, Macros & Reflection]] and follow the [[Learning Path — Unreal Engine Core|UE Core Learning Path]].

### Layer 5 — UE Networking

Multiplayer from the hardware up.

7 notes: Network Roles → RPCs → RepNotifies → PushModel → Iris Replication → Iris Filtering → GAS Networking.

Start with [[Network Roles]] and follow the [[Learning Path — Unreal Engine Networking|UE Networking Learning Path]].

### Layer 6 — Big O & Algorithms

6 notes: Complexity classes → Amortized analysis → Applied examples → Sorting algorithms → Graph algorithms → Dynamic programming.

Start with [[The Big O Complexity Spectrum]] and follow the [[Learning Path — Algorithms & Complexity|Algorithms Learning Path]].

---

## Reference

- [[Glossary]] — Every acronym defined (MMU, TLB, PTE, MESI, PLT, GOT...)
- [[Learning Path]] — All six category paths
- Use the right-side table of contents to jump between sections in any note
- Check the dashboard for recently viewed notes and quiz scores

---

DeepVault is built for Mayk's vault. All notes are source-verified against UE 5.8 engine code. 

### Moving Between Devices

Your quiz progress lives in your browser's localStorage. To continue studying on another device:

1. Export on your current device — downloads a JSON file
2. Transfer the file (email, AirDrop, cloud)
3. Import on the new device — merges all your scores

Export and Import buttons are at the bottom of the dashboard. No account needed.

---

Double-click index.html to launch. Everything stays on your device.
